structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a single local health system spanning 5 years (60 months). Data includes: structured demographics (age, sex, insurance), clinical notes (diagnosis codes, medication records, visit summaries), and patient-reported outcome measures (PHQ-9 or equivalent depression severity instrument) collected via validated survey instruments at clinical encounters. N ≈ 10,000 patients with complete labels. Data extraction period: months 1–60 of the EHR system.
structured_choices.label_definition: Depression severity score: the patient-reported outcome measure (e.g., PHQ-9 total score, range 0–27) collected at the time of or closest to a clinical encounter. Only the most recent validated severity score per patient within a 30-day window of the clinical encounter is used as the label to align timing. Scores are treated as continuous; no discretization into severity categories is performed at the feature engineering stage.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with patient-level stratification. Months 1–36 (first 3 years) are training data. Months 37–48 (year 4) are validation data. Months 49–60 (year 5) are held-out test data. This temporal ordering prevents information leakage: clinical and survey data from later months cannot influence training. Patient-level stratification (no single patient's records appear in multiple splits) prevents leakage from longitudinal patient trajectories. This temporal design is critical because: (1) depression severity scores evolve over time and future clinical information would not be available at prediction time, (2) the hypothesis tests predictive performance on unseen future time periods, not interpolation, and (3) model generalization to new patient cohorts is assessed only on held-out temporal data.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). The same fitted transformers are applied without refitting to validation (months 37–48) and test (months 49–60) data. This prevents data leakage: the model does not 'see' the statistics of validation or test data during preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed for comparison: (1) Transformer input (clinical notes + structured features): Clinical notes are tokenized and embedded using a pre-trained biomedical transformer (e.g., BioBERT or Clinical BERT). Structured features (age, sex, recent diagnosis codes as one-hot encoded indicators, medication classes) are separately embedded and concatenated with note embeddings. Temporal features (days since last visit, visit frequency in prior 90 days) are included. (2) Baseline input (traditional): Demographic features (age, sex, race/ethnicity), clinical features (count of depression-related diagnoses in past 12 months, antidepressant medication use yes/no, comorbidity count using Charlson index), and visit cadence features (days since last encounter, total visits in past year). No clinical notes are used in the baseline. All continuous features are standardized (z-score) using training set statistics. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing values are imputed using median (continuous) or mode (categorical) computed from the training set only. No feature engineering step encodes the label or uses future information; all features represent information available at the time of prediction.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: A fine-tuned pre-trained biomedical transformer (e.g., Clinical BERT) with a regression head (linear layer outputting a single continuous value). The transformer processes clinical notes; structured features are concatenated post-transformer. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set with the regression loss (mean squared error). Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, dropout rate, weight decay) are selected via validation set performance (see Validation Approach).
structured_choices.baseline: Linear regression (ordinary least squares or ridge regression) using only the traditional feature set (demographics + clinical features, no notes). This baseline represents the current state-of-practice in clinical prediction and is fair because: (1) it receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set (ridge penalty α selected to minimize validation MSE), (2) it uses the same train/val/test splits and preprocessing as the transformer, (3) it receives the same set of structured features that the transformer also receives (the additional advantage of the transformer is access to unstructured clinical notes). The baseline establishes whether the transformer's gains come from architectural superiority or merely from access to richer information (notes).
structured_choices.primary_metric: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the test set. RMSE is chosen because: (1) stakeholders need to identify patients at high depression risk, and RMSE penalizes large prediction errors (misidentifying low-risk or very-high-risk patients) more heavily than small errors, aligning with clinical risk stratification objectives, (2) depression severity is treated as a continuous outcome and RMSE is the natural fit for regression, (3) RMSE is in the same units as PHQ-9 scores (0–27), making clinical interpretation transparent. The difference in RMSE between transformer and baseline is the primary effect size.
secondary_metrics: Pearson and Spearman correlation between predicted and observed severity scores on the test set (measures monotonic agreement).
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set (less sensitive to outliers than RMSE; clinically meaningful as average prediction magnitude).
R² (coefficient of determination) on the test set (interpretability: proportion of variance explained).
Calibration curve on test set (predicted vs. observed mean severity in decile bins) to assess whether predicted scores align with actual outcomes.
Stratified performance: RMSE stratified by patient social determinants of health (race/ethnicity, insurance type, neighborhood SES if available) to detect bias.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 37–48). For the transformer: grid search over learning rates (1e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4), batch sizes (16, 32), and dropout rates (0.1, 0.3, 0.5) is conducted; the configuration minimizing validation RMSE is selected. For the baseline (ridge regression): grid search over ridge penalty α (10^-3 to 10^3 in log steps) is conducted; α minimizing validation RMSE is selected. The test set is not used during model selection; it is held out and evaluated exactly once at the end.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 49–60) is held completely separate and touched only once: after both models are fully trained and hyperparameters are finalized using validation data. Test set evaluation is performed on the final trained models with no further tuning, no data inspection, and no post-hoc adjustments to the feature set or model architecture. All reported results (RMSE, correlation, calibration) are computed on this held-out test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Social determinants of health (race/ethnicity, insurance type, neighborhood characteristics if available) are included as features in both the transformer and baseline models, allowing the models to adjust for these confounds. Stratified evaluation (RMSE reported separately for each racial/ethnic group and insurance type) is performed on the test set to detect differential model performance and assess whether predictions are biased. A sensitivity analysis is conducted: the model is retrained excluding notes (transformer → restricted transformer with notes ablated) to evaluate whether performance gains are driven by clinical notes or by confound adjustment in structured features. Temporal confounds (seasonal patterns in depression reporting, changes in clinical workflow over 5 years) are partially addressed by the temporal split: the model learns seasonal patterns in the training period and is tested on a future time period, assessing generalization to distribution shifts. Any systematic differences in patient demographics, depression prevalence, or data completeness between training, validation, and test periods are documented.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Depression Severity Prediction from EHR Data

### 1. Research Question and Scope

This experiment tests whether a transformer-based model fine-tuned on clinical notes and structured EHR data predicts depression severity scores with lower prediction error (higher accuracy) than traditional regression using only demographics and clinical features. Success is measured as statistically significant reduction in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on held-out test data. The experiment does not establish causation or mechanism; it measures predictive performance on a prospective-in-time hold-out set.

### 2. Data Strategy

The dataset comprises 5 years of EHR records from a single health system (approximately 10,000 patients). The label is the most recently recorded patient-reported depression severity score (e.g., PHQ-9, range 0–27) within 30 days of a clinical encounter, ensuring temporal alignment between clinical observations and the outcome measure. All data is extracted once; no real-time streaming or incremental updates are performed.

**Data quality handling:** Records with missing depression severity scores are excluded. Patients with fewer than one recorded outcome measure are excluded. Clinical notes with missing or truncated text are flagged; truncated notes are excluded from the analysis. Missing values in structured features (demographics, medication, comorbidities) are imputed using training set statistics (median for continuous, mode for categorical).

**Distribution alignment:** The local health system's patient population and data completeness over the 5-year period are documented. The hypothesis assumes that future deployment will occur within the same system or a similar population; significant distribution shifts (e.g., major changes in patient demographics or EHR documentation practices over the 5 years) are identified and reported.

### 3. Split Strategy and Temporal Design

Data is split temporally to prevent information leakage and to simulate prospective prediction:

- **Training:** Months 1–36 (first 3 years)
- **Validation:** Months 37–48 (year 4)
- **Test:** Months 49–60 (year 5)

All data at the patient level (i.e., all records for a single patient) are assigned to a single split; no patient appears in multiple splits. This prevents data leakage from longitudinal patient trajectories.

Temporal splitting is critical for this hypothesis because (1) depression severity evolves over time and future clinical information is unavailable at prediction time, (2) the model must generalize to unseen future time periods, not merely interpolate, and (3) confounds such as seasonal trends or evolving clinical practice are partially addressed by training on an early period and testing on a later one.

### 4. Feature Engineering

**Transformer model input:**
- Clinical notes (all encounter notes in the 12 months prior to the prediction date) are tokenized using a biomedical tokenizer (BioBERT or Clinical BERT) and embedded using the pre-trained transformer.
- Structured features (age, sex, race/ethnicity, recent diagnoses as one-hot encoded indicators, active medications by class, visit frequency, days since last visit) are separately embedded and concatenated with note embeddings.
- All continuous features are standardized using training set mean and standard deviation. Categorical features are one-hot encoded using training set vocabulary.

**Baseline model input:**
- Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity.
- Clinical features: count of depression-related diagnoses in the prior 12 months, current use of antidepressant medication (yes/no), Charlson comorbidity index.
- Visit cadence: days since last encounter, total visits in the prior 12 months.
- No clinical notes are used in the baseline.
- Features are standardized and encoded identically to the transformer model.

**Preprocessing:** All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36) and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents leakage.

No feature is constructed that encodes the label or uses information from after the prediction date.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A pre-trained biomedical transformer (e.g., Clinical BERT) is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. A linear regression head outputs a single continuous depression severity score. The model is trained using mean squared error (MSE) loss with standard backpropagation. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of fine-tuning epochs, dropout rate, weight decay) are selected via grid search on the validation set (see Section 6).

**Baseline model:** Ridge regression (linear regression with L2 penalty) trained on the traditional feature set. The ridge penalty α is selected via grid search on the validation set. Ridge regression is chosen to match the complexity-control effort applied to the transformer.

**Fairness of comparison:** Both models receive the same train/val/test splits, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning effort. The transformer's advantage is access to unstructured clinical notes; the baseline uses only structured features. A fair conclusion is that if the transformer achieves lower validation RMSE, the improvement comes from either (1) richer information in notes or (2) better feature learning via the transformer architecture. A secondary ablation (transformer without notes) isolates architectural effects.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 37–48):
- **Transformer:** Grid search over learning rates {1e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4}, batch sizes {16, 32}, and dropout rates {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}. The configuration minimizing validation RMSE is selected.
- **Baseline:** Grid search over ridge penalty α ∈ {10^-3, 10^-2, ..., 10^3}. The α minimizing validation RMSE is selected.

The test set is not touched during this process. After model selection, both models are evaluated exactly once on the held-out test set (months 49–60).

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the test set. RMSE is chosen because large prediction errors (e.g., missing a high-risk patient or incorrectly flagging a low-risk patient) are penalized more heavily, aligning with clinical risk stratification. RMSE is also interpretable in PHQ-9 units (0–27 scale).

**Secondary metrics:**
- Pearson and Spearman correlation (monotonic agreement between predicted and observed scores).
- Mean Absolute Error (average magnitude of prediction error).
- R² (proportion of variance explained).
- Calibration curve (predicted mean score vs. observed mean score by decile of predicted risk).
- **Stratified RMSE by social determinants** (race/ethnicity, insurance type) to detect bias.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

Social determinants of health (race/ethnicity, insurance type, neighborhood SES) are included as features in both models, allowing them to adjust for these confounds. Stratified test set evaluation reveals whether the model's predictions are systematically biased across demographic groups. A sensitivity analysis retrains the transformer without clinical notes; if performance degrades minimally, the improvement may be driven by confound adjustment rather than note information. Temporal confounds (seasonal trends, evolving clinical documentation) are partially addressed by the temporal split itself.

### 9. Test Set and Reporting

The test set is held completely separate and used only for final evaluation. All reported metrics (RMSE, correlation, calibration, stratified performance) are computed on the test set. Confidence intervals (95%) are computed via bootstrap resampling of the test set (with replacement, stratified by patient) to quantify uncertainty. The experiment will clearly state which population and time period the test results generalize to.
